narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 2.50, an LSAT score of 36, lower-middle family income background, and plans to attend full-time, along with your school decile rankings from Year 1 and Year 3 both sitting at the lowest 10th percentile. The admissions model, trained on data from thousands of past students' paths to the bar exam, predicted a 67% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try—high enough that it tipped the decision to reject your application, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to succeed and pass that critical hurdle. This prediction wasn't random; it hinged on a few key aspects of your profile that stood out the most to the model. The strongest influence pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking in the bottom 10%, well below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack 5th or 6th decile—this late-stage academic struggle signaled potential challenges in mastering the rigorous material needed for the bar. Your Year 1 decile, also in the bottom 10% compared to averages around the 5th or 6th, added to that concern as a second major factor, suggesting early patterns that carried forward. On a brighter note, your LSAT score of 36 was the top positive influence, nearly matching the dataset average of 36.7 and pulling the prediction somewhat toward success, showing your aptitude for logical reasoning under pressure. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your grades placed you in the lower tail of the distribution, while your LSAT kept you competitive, but those early and persistent low deciles outweighed it in the model's eyes. Your full-time status, shared by over 90% of students, and family income didn't sway things much here. This rejection stings, especially given the barriers many Hispanic men face in pursuing law, but it's not the end—consider retaking the LSAT to push it higher, bolstering your recent grades through targeted prep courses, or exploring post-bac programs to rebuild your academic deciles. Reach out to admissions for feedback, connect with mentors from similar backgrounds who passed the bar, and reapply stronger next cycle; your determination can shift this story.
